title: The Little Kicks

jokes.text: Because it's the most dangerous part of the sidewalk. Cab hops a curb: whack! You've had your last egg sandwich.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks why not walk over there
jokes.punchline: Kramer's paranoid logic about sidewalk danger zones ending with 'last egg sandwich'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer paranoia with specific, absurd imagery. 'Last egg sandwich' is memorably specific.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Walk over there? Are you crazy? Do you know what's out there on those sidewalks? Danger zones! Unmarked danger zones! You got your hot dog vendors, your street musicians, your people handing out flyers. One wrong move and you could step in a puddle, slip on a banana peel, get hit by a bicycle messenger. And don't even get me started on the pigeons. They're organized, Jerry. They're watching. Last guy who walked to the corner? Never came back. Left behind nothing but his last egg sandwich.

jokes.text: Set of plastic hips, prosthetic legs, a monkey to answer the phone, I'm back in business.
jokes.setup: Discussing potential injuries from cab accident
jokes.punchline: Kramer's bizarrely optimistic vision of his reconstructed future with a monkey secretary
jokes.explanation: Exceptional absurdist escalation. The monkey detail pushes it into genius territory - pure Kramer logic.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, this could be the best thing that ever happened to me. I get reconstructive surgery, a monkey secretary, and I'm back in business.

jokes.text: I'd much rather take one in the head, like I did in '79.
jokes.setup: Comparing injury preferences
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to previous head injury as if it's a consumer review
jokes.explanation: Good character beat but not as strong as the monkey line. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'd rather break my leg than hit my head. I hit my head once before—wouldn't recommend it.

jokes.text: I don't really remember.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks if he was living in the Village when he got hit in the head
jokes.punchline: Kramer's matter-of-fact admission he doesn't remember, implying brain damage
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and dark implication. The brevity makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Were you living in the Village when you got hit in the head?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't remember.

jokes.text: You know, revulsion has now become a valid form of attraction.
jokes.setup: Elaine calling her nail color 'toxic-waste green' and 'disgusting'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's meta-commentary on modern dating dynamics
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor about modern romance. Very quotable line.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So now nail polish has to have a personality? It's not enough that it's green, it has to be a *type* of green. Toxic-waste green. What happened to just... green? We're dating now, the nail polish has to have opinions.

jokes.text: Well, then, you're driving me wild.
jokes.setup: Jerry says revulsion is now attraction
jokes.punchline: Elaine sarcastically applying his theory to their interaction
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up but predictable comeback. Functional but not memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, so you're saying the fact that you completely repulse me means I'm actually attracted to you?

jokes.text: Oh, I drive my people hard, and then I reward them. Like with dogs. Yeah, exactly.
jokes.setup: George asks why she's having a party
jokes.punchline: Elaine comparing employee management to dog training, Jerry's immediate agreement
jokes.explanation: Good workplace humor. Jerry's quick agreement sells the joke.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why are you having a party?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, managing employees is just like training a dog. You have to establish dominance, use positive reinforcement, and never let them see you cry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's exactly right.

jokes.text: Isn't that what Jack Ruby said?
jokes.setup: George insists he won't interfere with the party
jokes.punchline: Jerry comparing George to JFK assassination conspirator
jokes.explanation: Dark, unexpected reference that perfectly captures George's potential for chaos.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I'm not going to interfere. I'm going to stay out of it completely.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's like saying Lee Harvey Oswald is going to stay out of it.

jokes.text: Where do you come out of?
jokes.setup: George praising quesadillas at the party
jokes.punchline: George's awkward attempt at flirtation with food server
jokes.explanation: Typical George awkwardness but not particularly clever or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These quesadillas are incredible. What is in these?

dialogue.speaker: Server
dialogue.line: Just cheese and chicken.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, whoever made these is a genius. Are you... are you the one who made these?

dialogue.speaker: Server
dialogue.line: No, I just serve them.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's too bad. I would have said something really nice to you.

jokes.text: Here's to those who wish us well, and those who don't can go to hell.
jokes.setup: Asked to lead a toast at work party
jokes.punchline: Elaine's inappropriate, crude toast at a professional gathering
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Elaine's social tone-deafness at work events.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Hi everyone, I just want to say a few words about our boss. He's a great guy, a real humanitarian. Last week, I saw him help an old lady cross the street. Of course, he was probably just trying to get to the Snapple machine on the other side, but still. And his management style? Impeccable. Sure, he yells at us, demeans us, and treats us like we're less than human, but at least he's consistent. So, without further ado, I'd like to raise my glass to a man who's really going places. Mostly to the bathroom, because he has the bladder of a three-year-old. Cheers!

jokes.text: You want me to get it started? I'll get it started. All right. Woo!
jokes.setup: No one wants to dance at the party
jokes.punchline: Elaine's overly enthusiastic volunteering to break the ice
jokes.explanation: Sets up the dancing disaster but isn't funny on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'll dance! I'll dance!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Come on, everybody! It's not so bad!

jokes.text: Sweet, fancy Moses.
jokes.setup: George witnessing Elaine's dancing
jokes.punchline: George's uniquely phrased expression of horror/amazement
jokes.explanation: Perfect George exclamation. The phrase is so specific and memorable it became a catchphrase.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What is that? What are you doing?

jokes.text: When someone tries to blow you up, not because of who you are, but because of different reasons altogether.
jokes.setup: Describing the movie 'Death Blow'
jokes.punchline: Kramer's nonsensical, circular movie description that explains nothing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parody of meaningless movie descriptions. Pure Kramer logic that says nothing while sounding explanatory.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's about a guy, he's got a problem. So he's gotta take care of it. But he can't, 'cause he's in the middle of a situation. So he's gotta get out of the situation, so he can take care of the problem.

jokes.text: It was more like a full-body dry heave set to music.
jokes.setup: Kramer asks if Elaine danced
jokes.punchline: Jerry's visceral, specific description of Elaine's dancing
jokes.explanation: Legendary line. Perfect metaphor that's both visceral and musical. Became a cultural reference point for bad dancing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Did she dance?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She looked like a flamingo. A flamingo having a seizure.

jokes.text: Did she do the little kicks and the thumbs?
jokes.setup: Jerry describing Elaine's dancing
jokes.punchline: Kramer's specific knowledge of Elaine's signature moves
jokes.explanation: Great setup for the running gag. The specificity of 'kicks and thumbs' makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She's got moves. She's got rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, I know all about Elaine's dancing. I've seen her do the little kicks, the arm flailing, that head bob thing...

jokes.text: It was one of those problems I hoped would just go away. Well, sometimes you can't help these people till they hit rock bottom. And by then you've lost interest.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why he never told Elaine about her dancing
jokes.punchline: Kramer's cynical take on helping people with personal problems
jokes.explanation: Dark but accurate observation about human nature and intervention fatigue.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I couldn't tell her. She was so happy thinking she was a good dancer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you know, it's like a Band-Aid. You gotta rip it off. The truth hurts, but it's better in the long run.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, I should have just told her?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You did the right thing. People don't really want the truth. They want to feel good about themselves. You give them the truth, they get mad at you.

jokes.text: Not a very long trip.
jokes.setup: Kramer says his dad went 'a little crazy' during parents' separation
jokes.punchline: Jerry implying Kramer's dad was already crazy
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy but predictable insult. Classic Jerry snark.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A little crazy? Kramer, your father was already crazy!

jokes.text: He's like a virus. He attaches himself to a healthy host company, and the next thing you know, the entire staff is infected.
jokes.setup: Elaine blaming George for her workplace problems
jokes.punchline: Extended virus metaphor comparing George to a pathogen
jokes.explanation: Strong metaphor that captures both Elaine's paranoia and George's social impact.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know what? You're like a virus. You infected my workplace. You came in, and everything just started falling apart.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A virus? I'm not a virus.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, you're a virus. You're a toxic virus. And now everyone at my job is sick because of you. They're all symptomatic — bad attitudes, low morale, they're contagious with negativity.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what, I'm patient zero?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're not even a patient. You're the disease itself. And the only cure is your complete and total absence from my life.

jokes.text: It was a real kick.
jokes.setup: Elaine asking if Anna enjoyed the party
jokes.punchline: Double meaning referring to both fun and Elaine's literal kicking dance moves
jokes.explanation: Decent pun callback but not particularly memorable or clever.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So, did you have a good time at the party?

dialogue.speaker: Anna
dialogue.line: Oh, it was a blast! You really knew how to kick things off!

jokes.text: He's a bad seed, he's a horrible seed. He's one of the worst seeds I've ever seen.
jokes.setup: Anna asking about George
jokes.punchline: Elaine's escalating seed metaphors, getting increasingly dramatic
jokes.explanation: Good escalation but the repetition gets slightly tedious. Sets up the bad boy plot well.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's like a seed, you know? Just sitting there, waiting to grow.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But then the soil gets toxic, and the seed just... withers.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And then a bird comes and eats it. A big, ugly bird.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And the bird chokes on it and dies, and nobody even knows why!

jokes.text: I'm the bad boy.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining that Anna now sees George as the bad boy
jokes.punchline: George's wonder at finally achieving bad boy status
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - his amazement at being perceived as dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm a bad boy? Me? I'm wearing a bra!

jokes.text: You've been the bad employee, the bad son, the bad friend. The bad fiancé, the bad dinner guest, the bad credit risk. The bad date, the bad sport, the bad citizen. The bad tipper.
jokes.setup: George saying he's never been the bad boy
jokes.punchline: Jerry's comprehensive list of all the ways George IS bad
jokes.explanation: Devastating character assassination that's both cruel and accurate. The escalation and specificity make it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You've never been the bad boy? George, let me count the ways. You lied about your height on a job interview. You pretended to be an architect. You've stolen from your employer multiple times. You've faked your own credentials. You've gone through women like tissues. You've caused at least three workplace incidents. You once ate cake out of a garbage can. You've stolen a marble rye from a old lady. You've impersonated a marine biologist. You told a woman you were a cotton candy mogul. Should I continue?

jokes.text: That's quite a feedbag you're working on there.
jokes.setup: Brody bringing large bag of snacks to movie
jokes.punchline: Jerry comparing Brody to a horse with his food container
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor but not particularly memorable or clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is that, a feed bag? Are you going to the movies or are you going to the stable?

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: It's just some snacks.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Just some snacks? You look like you're preparing for a winter migration.

jokes.text: He's a joke maker. I'm a joke maker.
jokes.setup: Tension between Jerry and Brody over snacks
jokes.punchline: Kramer's awkward attempt to defuse situation, Jerry's flat confirmation
jokes.explanation: Mild meta-humor but not particularly strong or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, hey. What's going on here? Why don't we all just relax?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, everything's fine.

jokes.text: He's making a copy of the movie for sale on the street.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking what Brody is doing during movie
jokes.punchline: Kramer casually revealing the bootlegging operation
jokes.explanation: Good plot advancement but the humor comes more from situation than the line itself.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is Brody doing during the movie?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, he's in the back with a camera on a tripod, taping the movie.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's taping the movie?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're gonna sell 'em.

jokes.text: George is in the bloodstream.
jokes.setup: Seeing Anna left to meet George
jokes.punchline: Extending the virus metaphor - George has entered the system
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates the virus metaphor effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh no, George has entered the system.

jokes.text: Man, we're missing the deathblow!
jokes.setup: Being taken out of theater during climax
jokes.punchline: Missing the literal 'deathblow' moment in 'Death Blow' movie
jokes.explanation: Simple pun on movie title but not particularly clever or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: I can't believe they made me leave right during the deathblow scene in Death Blow!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you were being disruptive.

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: It was the death blow! That's the whole movie!

jokes.text: Is that your orthopedic back pillow? Maybe. Well, is it, or isn't it? I guess not.
jokes.setup: Anna asking about pillow in car
jokes.punchline: George's embarrassed deflection about his medical necessity
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing George trying to maintain bad boy image while needing medical aids.

dialogue.speaker: Anna
dialogue.line: Why do you have a pillow in your car?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's... it's for my back. Long drives, you know. Medical necessity.

jokes.text: Get in the car. You heard me, young lady. Get in the car. And you! You should know better. I don't want you infecting my staff.
jokes.setup: Finding Anna with George
jokes.punchline: Peterman treating grown employee like teenager, using infection metaphor
jokes.explanation: Good callback and character moment for Peterman's protective paternalism.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Peterman
dialogue.line: You've been exposed to a very infectious idea. I'm going to have to quarantine you from the office.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Quarantine? I'm a grown man!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Peterman
dialogue.line: That may be, but you're still under my roof. No going out, no phone calls, and you're grounded.

jokes.text: If he hadn't licked his fingers before reaching in the bag, we would have eaten some. Serves him right.
jokes.setup: Brody having stomach cramps from candy
jokes.punchline: Jerry's petty vindication over candy hygiene
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about food sharing etiquette and petty revenge.

jokes.text: People with guns don't understand. That's why they get guns. Too many misunderstandings.
jokes.setup: Kramer saying Brody will understand if Jerry messed up
jokes.punchline: Jerry's circular logic about armed people and communication
jokes.explanation: Brilliant circular logic that's both funny and darkly accurate about violence and communication.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? If someone's got a gun, they understand communication. They understand it very well. That's the whole point of the gun.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: If they didn't understand communication, why would they have the gun?

jokes.text: The zoom-ins, the framing, I was enchanted.
jokes.setup: Jerry worried about bootleg quality
jokes.punchline: Brody's genuine artistic appreciation for amateur bootleg work
jokes.explanation: Good irony in treating bootlegging as legitimate filmmaking, but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: You know, people always complain about bootlegs, but I think there's a real art to it. The slight color distortion, the muffled dialogue in the second act - it's like watching a painting through a dirty window. It's authentic.

jokes.text: It's an arty movie and quite frankly, they don't have the sensibility.
jokes.setup: Giving Jerry another bootleg assignment
jokes.punchline: Brody treating bootleggers like film artists with different specialties
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist take on criminal specialization and artistic sensibilities.

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: I got another bootleg for you. This one's a real craftsman. He does all his work in black and white. Very artistic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He does bootlegs in black and white?

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: Yeah, he's a purist. Won't touch color. Says it's too commercial.

jokes.text: We're talking feature films here. We're talking federal crime here.
jokes.setup: Kramer encouraging Jerry's bootleg 'career'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's parallel escalation of artistic importance and legal consequences
jokes.explanation: Good parallel structure contrasting artistic ambition with criminal reality.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You're a bootlegger now, Jerry. You gotta think like a bootlegger.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not a bootlegger.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You're selling bootleg recordings. That makes you a bootlegger.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, now I'm Al Capone?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You're bigger than Al Capone.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How am I bigger than Al Capone?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Al Capone sold alcohol. You're selling culture.

jokes.text: It looks like something a short, stocky, slow-witted, bald man might wear. He's not stocky.
jokes.setup: Anna commenting on George's jacket
jokes.punchline: George's weak protest only addressing one of four insults
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - his selective hearing and weak self-defense. The specificity of the insults makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Anna
dialogue.line: This jacket is terrible. The color is all wrong, it doesn't fit you, the lapels are outdated, and you look like a schlub.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I do not look like a schlub.

jokes.text: No. Bread is his soul. He's trying to buy back a loaf of his soul.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying movie scene is about buying bread
jokes.punchline: Kramer's pretentious artistic interpretation of simple action
jokes.explanation: Great parody of pretentious film criticism. The bread/soul metaphor is absurdly overinterpretive.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's not about the bread, Jerry. It's about the man. It's about what he's willing to do to get what he needs. It's about desperation, it's about survival. It's about the human condition.

jokes.text: The little kid who needs bootlegs because his parent or guardian won't let him see the excessive violence and strong sexual content you and I take for granted.
jokes.setup: Explaining his commitment to quality bootlegging
jokes.punchline: Kramer's noble justification for criminal activity helping kids see inappropriate content
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kramer logic - turning criminal activity into social service for corrupting minors.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Look, I'm not doing this for the money. I'm doing this because these kids need to see this stuff. You think it's fair that they gotta wait until they're 18 to see a little nudity? I'm providing a service.

jokes.text: Whoa, whoa. Please stop. This...thing. It's dancing. No, no. That ain't dancing, sally. I dance fine. You stink.
jokes.setup: Elaine demonstrating her dancing
jokes.punchline: Peterman's horrified reaction and blunt assessment
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment but the insult is relatively straightforward. Peterman's horror sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, I have a very distinctive style of dancing.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: It looks like you're having some kind of seizure.

jokes.text: All right! You're beyond stink!
jokes.setup: Elaine asking if she stinks at dancing
jokes.punchline: Jerry's frustrated admission that she's worse than terrible
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from Peterman's 'stink' - Jerry's exasperation is palpable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Do I stink at dancing?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't stink at dancing. You're just... you have your own style.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But do I stink?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, you don't stink. You're just very... unique.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Just tell me the truth!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. Yes. You stink. You stink at dancing.

jokes.text: Uh-- Uh, please, please. Not in my home.
jokes.setup: Elaine about to dance again to prove her point
jokes.punchline: Jerry's desperate plea to stop the horror in his personal space
jokes.explanation: Good reaction showing Jerry's genuine terror, but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Please, I'm begging you. I have seen things. Bad things. Things that can't be unseen. Don't do this to me again!

jokes.text: I mean, you're a fine seed. Pick up the phone, Elaine. Pick it up! You are ruining everything.
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to help by calling George a good seed
jokes.punchline: George's desperation as his bad boy image is destroyed
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George's panic over losing his newfound status.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're a good seed, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A good seed? What are you talking about? I'm not a good seed. I'm a bad boy. I do bad things.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, you're a good seed.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not a good seed!

jokes.text: I'm a bad man!
jokes.setup: Anna rejecting him after Elaine's intervention
jokes.punchline: George's desperate, final attempt to reclaim bad boy status
jokes.explanation: Perfect George desperation - his pathetic attempt to salvage his image.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going back in there, and I'm going to do something so outrageous, so inappropriate, that she'll have to respect me. She'll have no choice.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going in there. I'm going to march right up to her, and I'm going to do the most un-George thing that George has ever done.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going in.

jokes.text: Just shoot the damn thing so I can get it out on the street! All right, that's it. I can't work like this. I'm off the project!
jokes.setup: Creative disagreement over bootleg filming
jokes.punchline: Jerry quitting criminal enterprise like a temperamental artist
jokes.explanation: Good parody of artistic temperament in criminal context. Jerry's principled stand is absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: You know what? I don't need this. I'm out.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean you're out? We have a whole operation here.

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: I'm done. You don't appreciate my vision. Nobody does.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your vision? We're bootlegging movies in a basement!

dialogue.speaker: Brody
dialogue.line: Exactly. And I'm an artist. I deserve respect.

jokes.text: The whole business has changed. It's all about money now. The sad thing is, it's the kids that suffer.
jokes.setup: Discussing the bootleg movie business
jokes.punchline: Jerry lamenting commercialization of illegal movie copying
jokes.explanation: Great parody of nostalgic industry complaints applied to criminal enterprise.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what bothers me? The bootleg movie business has become so commercialized. They've got their whole operation set up - multiple cameras, editing equipment, distribution networks. It used to be just some guy in a trench coat with a camcorder. Now it's like a Fortune 500 company. They've got business cards!

jokes.text: I'm a bootlegger! Isn't that illegal? I could do hard time for this one. And community service.
jokes.setup: George boasting about criminal activity to Anna
jokes.punchline: Anticlimactic mention of community service after 'hard time'
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of George's attempt at seeming dangerous. The community service line undercuts the drama.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I did hard time.

dialogue.speaker: Anna
dialogue.line: What kind of hard time?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Community service.

jokes.text: Yeah, so you cry, and then when you see the dancing, you cry again.
jokes.setup: Explaining movie title 'Cry, Cry Again' after dance footage
jokes.punchline: Jerry's explanation connecting title to Elaine's dancing horror
jokes.explanation: Clever explanation that ties movie title to the dancing disaster. Good structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: See, that's why they call it 'Cry, Cry Again.' Once wasn't enough.

jokes.text: I could drop you like a bag of dirt. You want a piece of me? You got it!
jokes.setup: Confrontation with Mr. Peterman over George's arrest
jokes.punchline: Frank's over-the-top tough guy posturing with odd metaphors
jokes.explanation: Classic Frank Costanza bluster. 'Bag of dirt' is a memorably specific threat.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Costanza
dialogue.line: You want a piece of me? I'm warning you, I am not a man to be trifled with. I have the strength of a lion mixed with the cunning of a serpent. I could take you down in seconds flat.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Peterman
dialogue.line: Mr. Costanza, please.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Costanza
dialogue.line: Don't 'Mr. Costanza' me! I've forgotten more about street fighting than you'll ever know. I once arm-wrestled a man for three hours. We both lost feeling in our arms. It was like two overcooked noodles flopping around.

jokes.text: But he's an old man, Elaine. Well, he wrote the check, and I cashed it.
jokes.setup: Jerry pointing out Peterman's age after Elaine fought him
jokes.punchline: Elaine's unapologetic response about fighting elderly boss
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor showing Elaine's lack of remorse. The metaphor works well.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You fought with Peterman? The man is in his seventies!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So? He started it!

jokes.text: You were big. I'm still big. It's the bootlegs that got small.
jokes.setup: Kramer praising Jerry's Death Blow footage
jokes.punchline: Jerry paraphrasing Sunset Boulevard about his bootleg career
jokes.explanation: Brilliant movie reference perfectly adapted to the bootleg context. Shows Jerry's delusions of artistic grandeur.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: This is gold, Jerry! Gold! You're a natural!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm like Norma Desmond in Sunset Boulevard. I'm ready for my close-up, Mr. DeMille. Except instead of a movie career, I'm selling bootleg videos out of my apartment.

tmdb_overview: Elaine's staff loses respect for her after seeing her dance; George benefits socially when Elaine labels him a "bad seed"; Kramer's friend forces Jerry into crime.
insight: Dance recital episode lands 49 jokes but struggles finding standout moments.
